original_abstract: Extracting structured knowledge from the vast body of unstructured scientific literature is a critical challenge for accelerating materials discovery. While traditional natural language processing (NLP) methods often focus on named entity recognition (NER), capturing the complex, hierarchical relationships between entities—such as the link between a specific chemical composition, its morphology, and its resulting properties—remains difficult. Here, we present a flexible approach to joint NER and relation extraction (RE) by fine-tuning pre-trained large language models (LLMs), specifically GPT-3 and Llama-2. We demonstrate that these models can be trained with relatively few human-annotated examples (approximately 100–600) to extract sophisticated scientific records and output them in structured formats like JSON or natural language. Our method was evaluated across three distinct materials chemistry tasks: identifying host-dopant relationships, cataloging metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and general materials information extraction. The results show that LLMs outperform traditional BERT-based pipelines in capturing complex relations, with the added benefit of providing built-in entity normalization and error correction. Furthermore, we show that using partially trained models in a human-in-the-loop workflow can reduce manual annotation time by over 50%. This approach offers an accessible and powerful framework for researchers to build specialized, high-fidelity databases from scientific text with minimal NLP expertise.
humanized_abstract: Scientific literature contains a wealth of knowledge about the structures and properties of materials. This knowledge can be extracted from scientific texts to accelerate the search for novel materials. However, conventional approaches in natural language processing (NLP) such as named entity recognition (NER) are not suitable to express the sophisticated, hierarchical relationships among the entities in scientific materials, for instance, the relationship between the chemistry of a material, its structure and its behavior. In this study, we present a flexible, approach to realize joint NER and relation extraction (RE) by fine-tuning pre-trained large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-3 and Llama-2. The LLMs can be trained with only about 100–600 instances of annotated text to extract advanced scientific contents from scientific texts and output them to structured formats such as JSON or other suitable formats in natural language. This approach was validated on three materials chemistry tasks, i.e., host dopant relationship extraction, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) extraction and general materials information extraction. It has been shown that the LLMs have better performance in extracting complex relations than the BERT-based models that are widely used in scientific information extraction. In addition, the LLMs benefit from the built-in entity normalization and post edit correction functionalities. We have also demonstrated that the partially pre-trained LLMs can be used in the human-in-the-loop mode to reduce the annotation time for the data scientists by over 50%. This approach not only facilitates the building of high-accuracy materials databases based on scientific literature but also requires minimal expertise in NLP.
